title: Rolling Stock Planning Using the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm
authors: Jiří Guth Jarkovský, Patricia Bickert, Elisabeth Wybo, Martin Leib
abstract: Rolling stock planning is a complex optimization problem in railway management that involves assigning physical trains to scheduled trips while minimizing operational costs. In this work, we address a specific instance of this problem featuring 190 trips over two days, subject to constraints such as mandatory maintenance stops. We reformulate the problem as a Maximum-Weight Independent Set (MWIS) problem on a graph where nodes represent feasible train cycles. To handle the computational complexity of the large search space, we propose a hybrid divide-and-conquer algorithm. This approach iteratively selects subgraphs and solves the MWIS problem using various solvers, including exact classical methods and the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA). We evaluate the algorithm's performance by comparing these methods and analyzing the scaling with respect to subgraph size, with QAOA assessed through both classical simulation and execution on a quantum device (IQM Emerald). Our results indicate that increasing the subgraph size generally improves solution quality, demonstrating that the hybrid framework can effectively bridge the gap between polynomial-time approximate solvers and exponential-time exact methods.
summary: This work applies quantum computing techniques, specifically QAOA, to solve the NP-hard rolling stock planning problem. By reformulating it as a Maximum Weight Independent Set problem, the authors propose a hybrid algorithm that uses divide-and-conquer strategies to tackle large instances. The results suggest that integrating near-term quantum solvers can effectively improve solutions over purely classical or approximate methods.
main_problem: The paper tackles rolling stock planning, a complex optimization problem in railway management that is mathematically formulated as finding a Maximum-Weight Independent Set (MWIS) on a large graph.
main_result: A hybrid quantum-classical framework was proposed, showing that increasing the size of subgraphs solved by QAOA generally improves solution quality compared to purely approximate methods.
method: The core method is a divide-and-conquer approach where the MWIS problem on large subgraphs is addressed using the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA), orchestrated by classical heuristics.
model_or_system: The system models a railway network with 190 trips over two days. The optimization graph nodes represent feasible train cycles, and edges connect conflicting cycles, leading to an MWIS formulation.
key_observables: Solution quality (minimizing empty kilometers) and resource consumption (number of trains required/fleet size).
important_parameters: Number of trips ($N=190$), maximum cycle length ($	ext{lmax}=4000 	ext{ km}$), QAOA depth parameter ($p$).
main_assumptions: The model assumes unlimited parking capacity and relies on a hybrid approach to manage the computational complexity, as exhaustive enumeration of cycles is prohibitive.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares solver performance for small subgraphs; Figure 2 shows that larger subgraphs lead to better solutions (less empty kilometers).
paper_structure: The paper first formulates the problem as MWIS. It then proposes a hybrid divide-and-conquer algorithm, detailing subgraph selection heuristics and applying QAOA on these smaller instances before iteratively updating the global graph.
why_interesting: It provides a concrete, real-world optimization application for near-term quantum algorithms like QAOA, demonstrating how quantum computation can be integrated into classical combinatorial problem-solving frameworks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Results showing the performance of various methods to solve the subgraph MWIS, with subgraph size kept fixed at 20 (and the greedy algorithm III-B1 as a benchmark). The left panel shows the data points from each individual full algorithm run, while the right panel shows the average performance of each method with standard error of the mean (SEM). IQM Emerald refers to results obtained from QAOA runs on IQM Emerald quantum computer. For more details about the solvers, see IV-B.

Fig. 2. Results showing the performance (measured by the number of empty kilometers) of the algorithm using the classical exact MWIS solver, as a function of the subgraph size. Linear fit of the data shows a clear trend of improving solution quality with increasing subgraph size.
